description: Baseline/default cultivar data for common garden crops. These provide fallback values (temp ranges, spacing, timing) for named varieties in cultivar-library.json that don't specify their own. Baseline entries do NOT appear in the Library tab on their own — to make a new crop browsable and plannable, you must also add at least one entry to cultivar-library.json.
howToAddACrop: 1. Add a baseline entry here with generic/typical values for the crop. 2. Add one or more named variety entries to cultivar-library.json (this is what users see in the Library tab). The library entry's 'crop' field must match the baseline's 'crop' field for fallback matching to work.

cultivars.notes: Cold-tolerant root vegetable. Can be sown 2-4 weeks before last frost. Harvest when roots are 1.5-3 inches. Greens are also edible. Typical maturity 50-65 days.
Requires trellising (6-10 ft). Longer harvest window than bush beans. Keep picked for continued production. Do not plant in cold soil. Typical maturity 60-90 days.
Compact plants, no trellising needed. Concentrated harvest period makes them good for canning/freezing. Do not plant in cold soil. Typical maturity 50-60 days.
Cool-season crop, frost tolerant to 20°F (-6°C). Shell for the peas inside. Plant 4-6 weeks before last frost. Stop producing when temps exceed 80°F (27°C). Typical maturity 60-70 days.
Edible pod with plump peas. Cool-season crop, frost tolerant. Plant 4-6 weeks before last frost. May need trellising (4-6 ft). Typical maturity 52-70 days.
Flat edible pod harvested before peas develop. Cool-season, frost tolerant. Plant 4-6 weeks before last frost. Typical maturity 55-65 days.
Long season crop (80-120 days). Needs warm soil (65°F/18°C). Harvest before frost, cure for storage. Includes butternut, acorn, delicata, spaghetti types.
Fast-maturing (35-55 days). Pick young and often for best flavor and continued production. Includes zucchini, yellow squash, patty pan types.
Bush habit, 3-4 ft tall. Concentrated harvest over 2-3 weeks. Good for canning, small spaces. Minimal pruning needed. Typical maturity 55-80 days from transplant.
Vining habit, 6-10+ ft tall. Continuous harvest until frost. Requires staking/caging and regular pruning. Typical maturity 70-100+ days from transplant.
Slow to germinate, needs warmth (75-90°F). Green to colored takes extra 2-3 weeks. Night temps below 55°F or above 75°F affect fruit set. Typical maturity 70-90 days from transplant.
Pick green or wait for red (more heat). Keep picked for continuous production. Slow germination needs warmth (75-90°F). Typical maturity 65-85 days from transplant.
Grown from seed potatoes. Plant 2 weeks before last frost when soil is 45°F (7°C). New potatoes can be harvested earlier. Early varieties: 60-80 days. Shorter storage life.
Grown from seed potatoes. Good balance of yield and storage. Tuber formation slows above 80°F (27°C). Mid-season varieties: 80-100 days.
Grown from seed potatoes. Best storage potatoes, keep 2-3 months. Late varieties: 110-135 days. Harvest when vines yellow. Cure before storage.
Long-day varieties for northern gardens. Start indoors 10-12 weeks before transplant. Bulbing triggered by day length. Cure for 2 weeks before storage. Typical maturity 90-120 days from transplant.
Often grown from sets (bulbs) rather than seed. Plant early spring or fall. Forms clusters of bulbs. Harvest when tops fall over. Cure before storage. Typical maturity 90-120 days.
Also called green onions or bunching onions. Cool-season, frost tolerant. Can succession plant spring through fall. Harvest at any size. Typical maturity 60-90 days.
Japanese bunching onion. Slender, upright, non-bulbing. Excellent for containers at tight spacing. Very cold-hardy, can overwinter in Zone 5+. Baby harvest in 30 days, full size in 60 days.
Very cold-hardy, tolerates frost. Bolts quickly in heat (above 75°F/24°C) and long days. Best as spring/fall crop. Seeds won't germinate above 85°F (29°C). Typical maturity 37-50 days.
Fastest lettuce type. Cut-and-come-again harvest. Seeds need light, don't bury deep. Bolts in heat; shade cloth helps. Baby greens in 25-40 days. Typical full maturity 40-50 days.
Upright heads, more heat tolerant than other head types. Seeds need light. Can harvest outer leaves or whole head. Typical maturity 55-75 days.
Soft, tender heads (Bibb, Boston types). Seeds need light. Less bolt-resistant than romaine. Typical maturity 50-65 days.

Warm-season crop, very frost sensitive. Direct sow when soil is 70°F (21°C). Pick regularly for continued production. Plants can produce for 6-10 weeks under good conditions. Trellising improves quality. Typical maturity 55-70 days.
Smaller fruits harvested young. Pick every 1-2 days at peak. Heavy producers but may exhaust faster than slicers. Plants typically produce for 4-6 weeks. Typical maturity 50-60 days.

Also called Chinese broccoli or kai lan. Harvest stalks with leaves and flower buds before flowers open. Side shoots develop after main harvest. Cool-weather crop, relatively cold-hardy for fall/winter harvests. Typical maturity 50-70 days.
Fast-growing root vegetable. Greens also edible and nutritious. Sow short rows every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest. Sweetens with light frost. Typical maturity 40-60 days.

Produces many small florets rather than one large head. Keep picked for continued production. Cool-season crop, temps below 45°F may cause bolting. Some varieties are biennial (overwintering). Typical maturity 55-80 days from transplant.
Hybrid of broccoli and gai lan. Tender asparagus-like stems with small florets. Sweet, mild flavor. Less cold-hardy than sprouting broccoli. Typical maturity 50-70 days from transplant.

Fast-growing peppery salad green. Baby greens ready in 21 days. Bolts quickly in heat; best as spring/fall crop. Sow every 3 weeks for continuous harvest. Very cold-hardy with protection. Typical maturity 30-40 days.

Perennial, does not bolt in hot weather like regular arugula. Narrower leaves, more intense flavor. Can harvest all summer. Slower to establish but longer productive life. Typical maturity 45-55 days.

Also called pac choi. Cool-season, grows quickly then bolts. Sow short rows every 2-3 weeks. Harvest before flower buds appear. Does not regrow after cutting. Tolerates light frost. Typical maturity 40-60 days.
Compact mini varieties (Toy Choi, etc.). Good for containers. Same culture as full-size but harvested earlier. Typical maturity 25-35 days.
Spicy, nutritious greens. Bolts in heat like spinach. Sow every 3 weeks spring/fall. Young leaves are milder. Excellent for microgreens. Tolerates light frost. Typical maturity 40-50 days.

Slow, erratic germination - keep soil moist. Sow shallowly (1/4 inch). Does not transplant. Loose, stone-free soil essential. Sweetens after frost. Baby carrots in 50 days. Typical maturity 60-80 days.

Slow-growing allium. Transplant when pencil-thick (20cm tall). Hill soil or mulch to blanch white stems. Very cold-hardy, can overwinter in mild climates. Harvest at any size. Typical maturity 90-120 days from transplant.

Hardy to Zone 7. Start indoors March-June, transplant for overwintering. Harvest late winter to early spring when little else is available. Mulch heavily in cold climates. Typical maturity 150-200 days.
Cool-season crop, tolerates light frosts. Harvest main head when tight; side shoots follow for 3-4 weeks. Head formation stalls above 75°F (24°C). Typical maturity 55-75 days from transplant.

Long-season crop, best grown for fall harvest. Sprouts mature bottom to top over 4-6 weeks. Frost improves flavor; tolerates 20°F (-7°C). Best when temps stay below 75°F (24°C). Typical maturity 80-110 days from transplant.
Very frost-tolerant, transplant 3 weeks before last frost. Harvest when head is firm; don't leave too long or heads split. Early varieties 60-70 days, storage types 80-100 days from transplant.

Most temperature-sensitive brassica. Tolerates light frost but not hard freezes. Heat above 80°F (27°C) causes poor curd formation. Blanch white varieties by tying leaves over the curd. Typical maturity 50-80 days from transplant.

Extremely cold-hardy, tolerates 20°F (-7°C). Flavor sweetens after frost. Harvest outer leaves continuously; plant produces for months. Among the hardiest garden vegetables. Typical maturity 50-65 days from transplant.
Fast-growing brassica. Harvest when bulb is 2-3 inches diameter; larger bulbs become woody. Tolerates light frost. Good for succession planting spring and fall. Typical maturity 38-60 days.

Fractal-patterned relative of cauliflower. Needs consistent cool temperatures for good head formation. Less cold-hardy than cabbage; transplant after last frost. Does not produce side shoots after harvest. Typical maturity 75-100 days from transplant.

Heat-loving crop, needs warm soil (70°F/21°C). Slow to start — begin indoors 8-10 weeks early. Fruits best when night temps stay above 60°F (16°C). Pick fruits glossy; dull skin = overripe. Typical maturity 60-80 days from transplant.

Includes cayenne, habanero, serrano, Thai types. Hotter varieties need longer season. Slow germination needs warmth (80-90°F). Stress (dry/hot) increases heat. Typical maturity 60-90 days from transplant.

Non-bell sweet peppers: banana, shishito, pimiento, etc. Similar culture to bell peppers but often slightly faster to mature. Pick regularly for continued production. Typical maturity 60-80 days from transplant.

Harvest when husk splits and fruit fills it. Needs 2+ plants for cross-pollination. Very productive once established. Sprawling plants, may need support. Self-seeds readily. Typical maturity 60-80 days from transplant.

Harvest when husked fruits drop to ground and turn golden. Sweet, tropical flavor. Sprawling plants 2-3 ft tall. Self-seeds prolifically. Related to tomatillo but sweeter. Typical maturity 65-75 days from transplant.
Compact winter squash, shorter season than butternut. Harvest when skin is dark green and hard. Shorter storage life (2-3 months) than other winter squash. Typical maturity 75-100 days.
Harvest when skin turns golden yellow and is hard. Stores 2-3 months. Vigorous vines need space (6-8 ft spread). Flesh separates into spaghetti-like strands when cooked. Typical maturity 90-110 days.
Harvest when skin is hard, stem is dry, and color is deep. Cure in sun 7-10 days for storage. Long vines need space (10-20 ft). Pie types 90-100 days; jack-o'-lantern types 100-120 days.

Also called muskmelon. Harvest when stem slips easily from fruit ('full slip'). Needs warm soil (70°F/21°C) and long hot season. Short-season varieties available for northern gardens. Typical maturity 75-95 days.
Longer season than cantaloupe. Does not slip from vine — harvest when blossom end yields to gentle pressure and skin turns creamy. Needs warm, sunny conditions. Typical maturity 90-110 days.

Needs warm soil (70°F/21°C) and long hot season. Ripe when tendril nearest fruit dries, belly spot turns yellow, and hollow sound when tapped. Icebox types (70-80 days) for short seasons; full-size 85-100 days.

Also called Mexican sour gherkin. Grape-sized fruits with cucumber-citrus flavor. Vigorous vines, benefits from trellising. Very productive once established. Pick at grape size; larger fruits become seedy. Typical maturity 60-75 days.
Fastest garden vegetable, 20-30 days. Sow every 1-2 weeks for continuous harvest. Bolts and turns pithy in heat. Best as spring/fall crop. Don't transplant. Typical maturity 22-30 days.

Includes daikon and watermelon radish. Much larger roots than spring radishes, needs 50-70 days. Best sown mid-to-late summer for fall harvest. Stores well in root cellar. Typical maturity 50-70 days.

Cross between turnip and cabbage. Larger and slower than turnips (80-100 days). Best sown mid-summer for fall harvest. Flavor sweetens after frost. Excellent storage root. Typical maturity 85-100 days.

Very slow, erratic germination — use fresh seed only. Deep, loose soil essential for long roots. Flavor sweetens dramatically after hard frost. Can overwinter in ground with mulch. Typical maturity 95-120 days.

Plant cloves in fall, 6-8 weeks before ground freezes. Needs cold vernalization. Harvest when lower leaves brown (mid-summer). Cut scapes in spring for bigger bulbs. Stronger flavor, fewer but larger cloves than softneck. 4-6 month storage. Typical maturity ~240 days (Oct to Jul).

Plant cloves in fall or early spring. More cloves per bulb than hardneck; milder flavor. Better storage life (6-9 months). Flexible stems allow braiding. Less cold-hardy than hardneck; better for mild climates. Typical maturity ~240 days fall-planted.

Also called Florence fennel or finocchio. Harvest when bulb is 3-4 inches across. Bolts readily in heat or if transplanted; best as fall crop. Hill soil around bulb to blanch. Does not transplant well — direct sow preferred. Typical maturity 60-80 days.

Grown from slips (rooted sprouts), not seed. Needs warm soil (65°F/18°C). Long, hot growing season required. Harvest before frost; cure at 85°F/80% humidity for 10 days, then store cool. Short-season varieties available. Typical maturity 90-120 days from transplant.

Heat-tolerant relative of beet (same species). Cut outer stalks at base; inner leaves continue growing. One of the longest-producing greens — spring through fall. Tolerates light frost. Colorful stems (Bright Lights). Typical maturity 50-60 days.

Among the most cold-hardy brassicas, tolerates 20°F (-7°C). Flavor sweetens after frost. Harvest lower leaves continuously. More heat-tolerant than kale — good summer green in moderate climates. Typical maturity 55-75 days.

Japanese mustard green with mild, peppery flavor. Fast growing; baby leaves in 21 days. More bolt-resistant than most Asian greens. Cut-and-come-again harvest. Cold-hardy with protection. Typical maturity 35-45 days.

Also called corn salad or lamb's lettuce. Extremely cold-hardy, one of the best winter greens. Mild, nutty flavor. Sow late summer for fall/winter harvest, or very early spring. Bolts quickly in warmth. Typical maturity 40-60 days.

Native North American green (Claytonia perfoliata). Mild, succulent flavor. Thrives in cool shade — ideal for spring/fall understory planting. Self-seeds readily. Goes dormant in heat. Typical maturity 35-45 days.

Not true spinach — a heat-loving substitute that thrives when spinach bolts. Soak seeds 24 hours before sowing (hard seed coat). Spreading ground-cover habit. Pick shoot tips for continued production. Typical maturity 50-65 days.
Also called summer crisp or French crisp. More heat-tolerant and bolt-resistant than butterhead. Crunchy texture between romaine and iceberg. Seeds need light. Typical maturity 50-60 days.

Tropical amaranth green, staple in Caribbean cooking. Thrives in heat when most greens bolt. Direct sow after last frost. Pick young leaves continuously. Nutritious, spinach-like flavor. Typical maturity 40-60 days.

Perennial herb with tangy, lemony flavor. Hardy to zone 3. Cut flower stalks to prolong leaf production. Harvest outer leaves continuously. One of the earliest spring greens. Typical maturity 50-70 days from seed, harvest year 1.

Demanding crop — needs consistent moisture, rich soil, and cool temps. Start indoors very early (10-12 weeks before transplant). Blanch stalks by hilling soil or wrapping. Bolt in temps above 80°F (27°C). Typical maturity 80-100 days from transplant.

Also called celery root. Knobby root with celery flavor, excellent in soups. Very long season — start indoors 10-12 weeks early. Needs consistent moisture. Harvest when root is 3-4 inches. Tolerates light frost; stores well. Typical maturity 100-120 days from transplant.

Plant in blocks (min 4x4) for wind pollination, not single rows. Needs warm soil (65°F/18°C). Harvest when silks brown and kernels exude milky juice. Sugar converts to starch quickly — eat soon after picking. Typical maturity 60-95 days.

Vigorous climber (8-12 ft) with ornamental flowers. Prefers cooler temps than common beans — flowers drop above 85°F (29°C). Pick young pods regularly; older pods become stringy. Perennial in mild climates. Typical maturity 55-70 days.

Heat-loving crop — thrives where others struggle. Soak seeds overnight for faster germination. Harvest pods at 2-3 inches; larger pods become tough and fibrous. Plants grow 3-6 ft tall. Typical maturity 50-65 days.

Ancient perennial potherb, hardy to zone 3. Slow to establish from seed — light harvest year 1, full harvest year 2+. Young shoots used like asparagus, leaves like spinach. Low maintenance once established. Typical maturity 80-100 days first year.

Tender annual herb, blackens below 40°F (4°C). Pinch flowers to prolong leaf production. Harvest by cutting stems above a leaf node for bushier growth. Genovese is the classic culinary type. Typical maturity 50-70 days.

Bolts very quickly in heat — best spring/fall. Sow every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest. Slow-bolt varieties (Calypso, Santo) extend production. Let some plants flower for coriander seed. Does not transplant well. Typical maturity 40-55 days for leaf.

Fast-growing herb, bolts in heat. Sow every 3 weeks for continuous leaf harvest. Fernleaf types are compact and slower to bolt. Flower heads used for pickling. Self-seeds freely. Does not transplant well (taproot). Typical maturity 40-55 days for leaf.

Very slow germination (2-4 weeks). Soak seeds overnight. Biennial — produces leaves year 1, flowers year 2. Cut outer stems at base for continuous harvest. Cold-hardy with protection. Italian flat-leaf and curly are main types. Typical maturity 70-90 days.

Hardy perennial herb, zone 3. Cut leaves to 2 inches above soil; regrows quickly. Divide clumps every 3-4 years. Purple flowers are edible. Easy to grow, pest-resistant. Typical maturity 60-90 days from seed; faster from divisions.

Hardy perennial herb, zone 4-5. Harvest just before flowers open for strongest flavor. Cut stems to 2 inches; regrows vigorously. Dries well. Greek oregano has best culinary flavor. Spreads — can become invasive. Typical maturity 70-90 days from seed.

Hardy perennial herb, zone 4-5. Very slow from seed — transplants or divisions preferred. Needs well-drained soil; dies in wet winter conditions. Woody subshrub, prune lightly in spring. Harvest sprigs anytime. Typical maturity 85-95 days from seed.

Hardy perennial herb, zone 4-5. Woody shrub, replace every 3-5 years as plants get leggy. Needs well-drained soil. Don't cut into old wood. Harvest lightly first year. Dries and freezes well. Typical maturity 75-90 days from seed.

Tender perennial, hardy to zone 7-8. Bring indoors in cold climates. Very slow from seed (low germination); cuttings are easier. Needs excellent drainage. Woody shrub, prune regularly. Can harvest sprigs year-round in mild climates. Typical maturity 80-100 days from seed.

Very hardy perennial, zone 3-4. Extremely invasive — grow in containers or with root barriers. Divisions/cuttings grow faster than seed. Cut frequently for bushier growth. Many types: spearmint, peppermint, chocolate, etc. Typical maturity 80-95 days from seed.

French tarragon (superior flavor) must be grown from divisions — it does not produce viable seed. Russian tarragon grows from seed but has inferior flavor. Hardy perennial, zone 4-5. Divide every 3-4 years. Typical maturity 60-90 days from division.
Tender perennial, grown as annual in cold climates. Sweeter and more delicate than oregano (close relative). Harvest before flowering for best flavor. Dries well. Typical maturity 55-70 days.

Annual herb, more delicate flavor than winter savory. Traditional 'bean herb' — classic pairing with green beans. Harvest before flowering. Easy to grow from direct sow after last frost. Typical maturity 55-65 days.

Hardy perennial, zone 5-6. Stronger, more peppery than summer savory. Woody subshrub, prune in spring. Slow from seed. Drought tolerant once established. Evergreen in mild climates. Typical maturity 75-90 days from seed.

Hardy perennial, zone 4. Lemon-scented leaves for tea and cooking. Very easy to grow; self-seeds aggressively. Cut back hard in midsummer for fresh regrowth. Related to mint, similarly invasive. Best flavor before flowering. Typical maturity 60-80 days.

German chamomile (annual) is the common tea herb. Tiny seeds need light to germinate — surface sow. Harvest flower heads when petals fold back. Self-seeds freely. Drought tolerant. Roman chamomile is a perennial ground cover. Typical maturity 55-65 days.

Cool-season herb with delicate anise-parsley flavor. French cuisine staple (fines herbes). Bolts very quickly in heat — grow in partial shade, spring/fall only. Does not transplant or dry well. Use fresh. Typical maturity 45-60 days.

Also called perilla or Japanese basil. Green and purple-leaf varieties. Seeds need light and cold stratification (refrigerate 1 week). Used in Japanese, Korean, Vietnamese cuisine. Self-seeds readily. Typical maturity 55-70 days.

Tropical grass, grow as annual in cold climates. Easiest to start from store-bought stalks rooted in water. Large clumps (3-5 ft). Harvest outer stalks when 1/2 inch thick. Bring indoors before frost. Typical maturity 90-120 days from transplant.

Natural sweetener herb. Poor seed germination; buy transplants. Tender perennial (zone 9+), grow as annual. Harvest leaves before flowering for highest sweetness. Pinch flowers. Needs well-drained soil. Typical maturity 80-100 days from transplant.

Very large perennial herb (3-6 ft tall), zone 3-4. Intense celery flavor — use sparingly. Harvest young leaves and hollow stems. Dies back in winter, returns spring. One plant is enough for most gardens. Typical maturity 80-100 days from seed.

Grown from root cuttings, not seed. Plant 4-6 weeks before last frost. Extremely vigorous perennial, zone 3 — can become invasive. Dig roots in fall after frost. Pungency is strongest when freshly grated. Leave some root in ground for next year. Typical maturity 140-160 days.

Long-lived perennial (15-30 years), zone 3. Usually planted from 1-year crowns. Do NOT harvest year 1; light harvest year 2; full harvest year 3+. Harvest spears when 6-8 inches tall for 6-8 weeks in spring. All-male hybrids are most productive.

Very hardy perennial, zone 2-3. Usually planted from divisions/crowns. Do not harvest year 1. Harvest stalks by pulling and twisting (don't cut). LEAVES ARE TOXIC (oxalic acid) — eat stalks only. Needs winter chill; struggles in hot climates. Pull seed stalks. Produces for 10-15 years.

Usually planted as bare-root crowns, not from seed. June-bearing: one large crop in early summer. Day-neutral: smaller fruit all season. Remove flowers year 1 (June-bearing) for bigger year 2 crop. Mulch with straw. Hardy perennial, zone 3-4. Renovate beds every 3-4 years.

Heat-loving annual flower. Cut-and-come-again — more you cut, more blooms. Direct sow is preferred; transplants can be finicky. Excellent cut flower. Susceptible to powdery mildew; space for air flow. Typical first bloom 55-70 days from sow.

Easy annual flower, blooms until frost. Thrives in poor soil — rich soil produces more foliage, fewer flowers. Tall varieties (4-6 ft) may need staking. Deadhead for continuous bloom. Self-seeds. Attracts pollinators. Typical first bloom 55-65 days from sow.

Plant corms 10-15 cm deep, pointy side up, after last frost. Stagger plantings every 2 weeks for continuous blooms. Tall varieties need staking. Dig up corms before first fall frost in cold climates. 70-90 days to first bloom.

Cool-season annual. Thrives in spring and fall; fades in summer heat above 24°C. Very frost-tolerant — can handle light freezes. Start indoors 8-10 weeks before transplanting, or buy starts. Deadhead for continuous bloom. Typical first bloom 60-80 days from sow.

Warm-season annual. Blooms continuously until frost. Spreading/trailing types excellent for containers and hanging baskets. Deadhead or shear back mid-season if leggy. Typical first bloom 60-80 days from sow.

Cool-season annual, excellent cut flower. Thrives in spring and fall; struggles in summer heat above 27°C. Tolerates light frost. Pinch seedlings at 4-6 leaf stage to encourage branching. Surface sow — seeds need light to germinate. Typical first bloom 70-90 days from sow.

Heat-loving annual with dramatic trailing rope-like burgundy flower tassels up to 60 cm long. Drought-tolerant once established. Plants reach 90-120 cm tall. Direct sow preferred — transplants can be touchy. Edible leaves and seeds. Makes a striking dried flower. Typical first bloom 65-75 days from sow.

Low-growing (10-15 cm) fragrant annual groundcover. Tolerates light frost. Blooms prolifically spring through fall; may pause in peak summer heat then rebloom. Attracts beneficial insects. Self-sows readily. Surface sow — seeds need light. Shear back mid-season if leggy to encourage rebloom. Typical first bloom 45-55 days from sow.
